title: Node Server Deployment: Beyond npm start
excerpt: Your Express app works on localhost. Here's how to make it work everywhere else without the midnight panic calls.
meta_title: Node.js Server Deployment Guide - Production Ready Setup
meta_description: Deploy Node.js servers that don't crash at 3am. PM2, clustering, monitoring, and all the things tutorials skip.
author: Brett & Tibbe
content:
Every Node tutorial ends with npm start. That's where your real problems begin.

We've kept Node servers running for years without 3am panic calls. Here's how.

PM2: Your Process Manager That Actually Manages

Forget nodemon in production. PM2 is what you want:

npm install -g pm2
pm2 start app.js --name "my-api"
pm2 startup
pm2 save

But the real power is in ecosystem.config.js:

module.exports = {
  apps: [{
    name: 'my-api',
    script: './app.js',
    instances: 'max',
    exec_mode: 'cluster',
    env: {
      NODE_ENV: 'production',
      PORT: 3000
    },
    error_file: './logs/err.log',
    out_file: './logs/out.log',
    log_file: './logs/combined.log',
    time: true,
    max_restarts: 10,
    min_uptime: '10s'
  }]
}

Clustering: Free Performance

Node is single-threaded, but your server doesn't have to be. PM2's cluster mode spawns one process per CPU core.

instances: 'max' uses all available cores. Start with that.

Gotcha: Shared state breaks in cluster mode. Use Redis for sessions, not memory.

Environment Variables: Do It Right

Create .env.production:

NODE_ENV=production
PORT=3000
DB_URL=postgresql://...
JWT_SECRET=your-super-secret-key
REDIS_URL=redis://...

Load them with dotenv, but only in development:

if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production') {
  require('dotenv').config()
}

In production, set env vars in your deployment system, not .env files.

Health Checks That Matter

Every production server needs health endpoints:

app.get('/health', (req, res) => {
  res.json({
    status: 'OK',
    timestamp: new Date().toISOString(),
    uptime: process.uptime(),
    memory: process.memoryUsage()
  })
})

app.get('/readiness', async (req, res) => {
  try {
    // Check database connection
    await db.raw('SELECT 1')
    res.json({ status: 'ready' })
  } catch (error) {
    res.status(503).json({ status: 'not ready' })
  }
})

Logging: Your Future Self Will Thank You

console.log is not logging. Use Winston or Pino:

const winston = require('winston')

const logger = winston.createLogger({
  level: 'info',
  format: winston.format.json(),
  transports: [
    new winston.transports.File({ filename: 'error.log', level: 'error' }),
    new winston.transports.File({ filename: 'combined.log' })
  ]
})

// In development, also log to console
if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production') {
  logger.add(new winston.transports.Console({
    format: winston.format.simple()
  }))
}

Memory Leaks: The Silent Killer

Node servers crash from memory leaks, not traffic spikes. Monitor memory usage:

// Add to your health endpoint
app.get('/health', (req, res) => {
  const usage = process.memoryUsage()
  const mb = (bytes) => Math.round(bytes / 1024 / 1024)
  
  res.json({
    status: 'OK',
    memory: {
      rss: `${mb(usage.rss)} MB`,
      heapUsed: `${mb(usage.heapUsed)} MB`,
      heapTotal: `${mb(usage.heapTotal)} MB`
    }
  })
})

The Docker Way

If you're containerizing (and you should be):

FROM node:18-alpine
WORKDIR /app

# Copy package files first (better caching)
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --only=production

# Then copy app code
COPY . .

# Don't run as root
USER node

CMD ["npm", "start"]

What Breaks in Production (and How to Fix It)

From our war stories:

  • File uploads fill disk space. Set size limits and clean up temp files.
  • Database connections timeout. Use connection pooling and handle reconnection.
  • CORS breaks your frontend. Configure it properly from the start.
  • SSL certificates expire. Use Let's Encrypt with auto-renewal.
  • Dependencies have security issues. Run npm audit regularly.

Deploy Node servers that stay running. Your sleep schedule depends on it.
